content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Alexandros Giannakakis on UnsplashLast Tuesday I watched GitHub Copilot generate forty lines of perfect React code in about four seconds.
My stomach dropped.
I sat there staring at my screen like someone had just told me my dog could suddenly drive a car. Impressive? Absolutely. Terrifying? Also absolutely.
This is the moment every software engineer has had in the past eighteen months.
That cold realization that something fundamental shifted.
But here’s what happened next.
I copied that perfect code into my project.
It broke everything.
Not because the code was wrong. It was technically flawless. Beautiful even. The kind of clean, functional code that makes you nod appreciatively.
But it was solving the wrong problem.
I’d asked AI to build me a bridge. It built me a beautiful suspension bridge with golden cables and perfectly engineered supports.
I was trying to cross a puddle.

The Fear Is Real (And That’s Okay)
Let’s be honest with each other.
Every developer I know has had at least one sleepless night thinking about AI replacing software engineers.
The headlines don’t help.
“AI Will Replace Programmers by 2027”
“Software Engineering Is Dead”
These aren’t subtle. They’re designed to make you anxious. To get clicks. To sell courses teaching you how to survive the AI apocalypse.
And they work.
I’ve been writing code for over a decade. I’ve survived three major “this will kill programming” revolutions.
The cloud was going to replace IT. Mobile was going to replace web. No-code was going to replace everything.
None of them did.
But AI feels different.

Why This Fear Exists
The fear isn’t irrational.
ChatGPT can explain complex algorithms better than most senior engineers.
Cursor can refactor an entire codebase while you watch.
Copilot autocompletes entire functions like it’s reading your mind.
We’re not talking about simple automation anymore.
We’re talking about something that feels… intelligent.
And when something feels intelligent, we instinctively compare it to ourselves.
That comparison is uncomfortable.
Because let’s face it — many of us spent years learning syntax, memorizing design patterns, and building our careers on the ability to write code.
Now anyone with a $20/month subscription can generate that same code in seconds.
If that’s all there was to the job, we’d be in trouble.
But it’s not.

What AI Is Genuinely Amazing At
Look, I’m not here to downplay what these tools can do.
They’re incredible.
Let me tell you what I actually use them for every single day.
Boilerplate code
I haven’t written a basic CRUD endpoint from scratch in months. Why would I? It’s tedious, repetitive, and AI handles it perfectly.
Explaining code
When I inherit a legacy codebase written by someone who clearly hated future developers, I drop the worst functions into Claude and ask “what is this doing and why does it hurt me?”
Documentation
Writing docs is my least favorite part of the job. AI doesn’t complain about it.
Unit tests
“Write tests for this React component” saves me about an hour per feature.
Refactoring
“Take this 200-line mess and make it readable” is a magical prompt.
SQL queries
I still can’t remember if it’s LEFT JOIN or RIGHT JOIN without thinking about it for five seconds. AI remembers for me.
Debugging assistance
This one deserves its own paragraph.

The Debugging Story That Changed Everything
A few months ago, I spent six hours debugging a production issue.
The bug was subtle. Intermittent. The kind that makes you question your sanity and career choices.
I fed all the logs, error messages, and relevant code to every AI tool I could find.
ChatGPT gave me three theories. All plausible. All wrong.
Claude suggested five potential fixes. I tried them all. Nothing.
Copilot couldn’t even autocomplete my desperate search queries.
Eventually I found the issue myself.
It was a race condition that only appeared when a specific database index was stale, combined with a caching layer that wasn’t invalidating properly, triggered by a user action that happened maybe twice a day.
AI never stood a chance.
That’s when I realized something important.
AI is amazing at solving problems you can clearly define.
It’s terrible at figuring out what the problem even is.
And that’s the whole game.

What AI Still Struggles With
Let me be direct about the limitations.
Messy business requirements
“Build a feature that helps users feel more engaged but also doesn’t annoy them and also fits within our brand voice and also works on all devices and also can be built in two weeks.”
Good luck, AI.
Architecture decisions
Should we use microservices or a monolith? Should we go serverless or use containers? Should we rewrite or refactor?
These depend on team size, budget, timeline, existing infrastructure, and fifty other factors AI can’t possibly weigh.
Trade-offs
Every engineering decision is a trade-off.
Performance versus readability. Speed versus quality. Features versus stability.
AI can’t make these judgments because they’re contextual and often emotional.
Product thinking
Does this feature actually solve the user’s problem? Or are we just building something cool?
Handling ambiguity
Requirements change. Stakeholders change their minds. The market shifts.
AI works with what you give it. It doesn’t adapt to chaos.
Communication
Explaining technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders. Negotiating priorities. Saying “no” gracefully.
Owning production systems
When your system fails at 2 AM, who wakes up to fix it?
Accountability
When things go wrong, who takes responsibility?
Hint: It’s not the AI.

The Real Job of a Software Engineer
Companies don’t hire programmers because they type code.
They hire people who solve business problems.
Think about that for a second.
Code is just the medium.
The actual value is in understanding what needs to be built, why it matters, and how to build it in a way that serves the business and its users.
AI generates code.
Humans decide which code should exist.
And more importantly, they decide what shouldn’t exist.

A Realistic Example
Picture two developers.
One is junior. Fast with Cursor. Impressed by what AI can generate. They copy suggestions directly into production without much thought. Code works? Ship it.
The other is senior. They’ve been burned before. They know that every line of code is a liability. They ask questions.
“Why are we building this feature at all?”
“Does this actually solve the user’s pain point?”
“What’s the cost of maintaining this?”
“What happens when we need to change it later?”
The senior developer will delete more code than they write. They’ll reject features that don’t make sense. They’ll push back on AI suggestions that are technically correct but strategically wrong.
That’s the value.
And AI can’t replicate it.

How AI Changed My Workflow (Without Replacing Me)
Let me walk you through how my actual day looks now.
Morning
I open my IDE with Cursor. I have a rough idea of what I want to build. I start typing comments describing what the code should do.
Cursor autocompletes entire functions.
I read them carefully. Make adjustments. Sometimes I use the suggestions. Sometimes I ignore them entirely.
Midday
I have a bug. I’m stuck. I paste the error into ChatGPT and ask for help. It gives me a starting point. Sometimes it’s correct. Sometimes it sends me down the wrong path.
I figure out which.
Afternoon
I need to write documentation for a complex API. I feed the code to Claude and ask it to draft the docs. I edit heavily, adding context and examples that make sense for our specific users.
End of day
I’m reviewing a pull request. The code is clean. Well-formatted. Probably written with AI assistance.
But I notice the developer didn’t think through the edge cases.
I leave a comment: “What happens if the database is empty? What if the user is logged out? What if the API rate limits?”
The developer responds: “Oh shoot, I didn’t think about that.”
That’s the gap.
AI writes code.
Humans think about what could go wrong.

What Actually Gets You Replaced
I need to be blunt about something.
AI won’t replace you because it’s better at coding.
It will replace you if you refuse to change.
Here’s what actually gets people replaced:
Refusing to learn AI tools
Not using ChatGPT, Copilot, or Cursor in 2026 is like refusing to use Google in 2005. It’s technically possible. You’re just intentionally making yourself slower.
Only knowing syntax
If all you can do is translate requirements into code, you’re replaceable. AI is better at that now.
Never understanding systems
Knowing how to write a function is basic. Understanding how that function fits into a distributed system, handles load, and fails gracefully — that’s the real skill.
Being unable to communicate
If you can’t explain what you’re doing to non-technical people, you’re limiting your value dramatically.
Never learning business context
Why does this company exist? Who pays us? What do our users actually need? If you don’t know these things, you’re just a code generator.

The Way I Think About It Now
Here’s my honest take.
Software engineering has changed.
It’s less about writing code and more about deciding what code to write.
It’s less about syntax and more about systems.
It’s less about being a programmer and more about being a problem solver.
The barrier to entry has lowered.
Anyone can generate code now.
But the bar for excellence has gone up.
Because now that everyone can generate code, the question is: can you figure out what’s worth generating?

I’ve been thinking about this a lot lately.
What makes me valuable? What makes any of us valuable?
It’s not the typing.
It’s the thinking.
It’s the judgment.
It’s the ability to navigate ambiguity and uncertainty.
It’s knowing when to say “this code is clever but we shouldn’t ship it.”
It’s knowing when to say “this approach won’t work because I’ve seen it fail before.”
It’s knowing when to say “the user doesn’t need this feature, they need this other thing instead.”
AI will never replace those moments.
Because those moments come from experience.
They come from failure.
They come from understanding that code is the easy part.
Everything else is the hard part.
And everything else is what makes us valuable.

One More Thing
I still get nervous when I see AI generate beautiful code in seconds.
That feeling hasn’t gone away.
But now I understand it differently.
That nervousness isn’t fear of being replaced.
It’s recognition that the game has changed.
And I’d better change with it.
So I use AI every day.
I let it write the boilerplate.
I let it suggest improvements.
I let it handle the tedious stuff.
And then I spend my energy on the things that actually matter.
The architecture.
The trade-offs.
The human conversations.
The thinking.
The things AI can’t do.

Here’s The Truth
AI will kill some tech jobs.
The ones that were always just about writing code.
The ones where people never learned the business context.
The ones where communication was ignored.
The ones where system design was an afterthought.
Those jobs were already on thin ice.
AI just made the ice visible.
But for everyone else?
The people who think deeply about what they’re building?
The people who care about users?
The people who can navigate uncertainty?
The people who communicate clearly and lead effectively?
There will be more opportunity than ever.
Because now we have tools that handle the grunt work.
And we can focus on what matters.

Don’t let AI replace you.
Let it upgrade you.
